text: The surgeon begins the standard dissection, focusing on the lateral aspect of the artery with minimal visible inflammation. The peritoneum is dissected higher on the gallbladder, mobilizing the artery and developing the lateral peritoneal dissection.

The surgeon continues to dissect the peritoneum on the lateral aspect of the cystic artery, a technique referred to as cystic artery mobilization of Erich Naur, to enhance the surgical field for a more effective operation.
The surgeon continues dissection underneath the gallbladder on the lateral side, revealing a twisted cystic duct that is being straightened through careful manipulation.
The surgeon uses a hook cautery to precisely dissect the tissue between the cystic artery and duct, highlighting the efficiency required in robotic surgery due to limited instrument exchanges.
The surgeon works towards achieving the critical view of safety, carefully dissecting off the cystic plate to prepare for the placement of clips and transection of the cystic duct and artery.

The surgeon extends the dissection higher on the cystic plate, achieving the critical view of safety with two distinct structures visible entering the gallbladder. Clips are applied to these structures in preparation for transection.
The surgeon performs the transection from the console using robotic monopolar scissors, due to the absence of an experienced assistant at the bedside.
